coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of efficiency and stability in organic solar cells by employing MoS2 transport layer, graphene electrode, and graphene quantum dots-added active layer
coredata.dc:creator: Shin, Dong Hee
Jang, Chan Wook
Ko, Jung Sun
Choi, Suk-Ho
coredata.dc:description: For enhancing efficiency and flexibility of organic solar cells, new constituent materials are highly required. Here, we first report flexible organic solar cells (FOSCs) by employing MoS2 hole transport layer (HTL), bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)-amide-doped graphene (TFSA-GR) transparent conductive electrode (TCE), and GR quantum dots (GQDs)-added active layer. Power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the FOSCs without GQDs strongly depends on number of layers (Ln) of MoS2 as well as on doping concentration (nD) of TFSA-GR, thereby showing maximum PCE of 3.56% at Ln = 2 and nD = 20 mM, resulting from the lowest resistance at the TCE/MoS2/active layer interfaces. The long-term stability of the FOSCs is almost two times better than that of their counterparts with conventional organic HTL. By adding GQDs in the active layer, the PCE is further enhanced to 4.23% and is maintained at 89/82% of the original value after inner/outer repeated bending tests for 1000 cycles, respectively, indicating outstanding mechanical stability. These results suggest that GQDs are very useful for the active layer of FOSCs with better PCE and flexibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2
Graphene
Graphene quantum dot
Organic solar cell
Flexibility
Stability

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (No. 2020R1A2C2004376).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic structure/real image and (b) energy band diagram of a typical FOSC with a structure of TFSA-GR TCE/MoS2 transport layer/P3HT:PCBM active layer/Al. The arrows in (b) indicate shifts of Fermi levels in TFSA-GR and MoS2 depending on Ln and nD. (c) Photo J-V curves and (d) EQE spectra of the FOSCs (nD = 20 mM) without GQDs for various Ln.
(a) Dark I-V characteristics of the FOSCs for various Ln, divided in three voltage regions. (b) Extracted Rsh, n, and Rse parameters of the FOSCs as functions of Ln.

(a) Photo J-V curves, (b) EQE spectra/integrated Jsc, and (c) dark I-V curves of the FOSCs with/without GQDs. (d) Nyquist plots of the FOSCs with/without GQDs. Here, the EIS was measured in the dark condition and the equivalent electrical circuit was shown in the inset.

Evolution of J-V curves throughout 1000 bending cycles at d = 4 mm for the FOSCs under (a) inner bending and (b) outer bending. (c)-(d) Changes of normalized PCEs as functions of bending cycles at d = 4 mm for the FOSCs under (c) inner bending and (d) outer bending. The insets describe the bending tests.
Photovoltaic parameters of FOSCs without GQDs for various Ln at nD = 20 mM.
Photovoltaic parameters of FOSCs with/without GQDs at Ln = 2 and nD = 20 mM.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of efficiency and stability in organic solar cells by employing MoS2 transport layer, graphene electrode, and graphene quantum dots-added active layer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Andong National University, Andong, Gyeongbuk 36729, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Andong National University, Andong, Gyeongbuk 36729, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics and Institute of Natural Sciences, Kyung Hee University, Yongin 17104, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics and Institute of Natural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics and Institute of Natural Sciences, Kyung Hee University, Yongin 17104, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For enhancing efficiency and flexibility of organic solar cells, new constituent materials are highly required. Here, we first report flexible organic solar cells (FOSCs) by employing MoS2 hole transport layer (HTL), bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)-amide-doped graphene (TFSA-GR) transparent conductive electrode (TCE), and GR quantum dots (GQDs)-added active layer. Power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the FOSCs without GQDs strongly depends on number of layers (Ln) of MoS2 as well as on doping concentration (nD) of TFSA-GR, thereby showing maximum PCE of 3.56% at Ln = 2 and nD = 20 mM, resulting from the lowest resistance at the TCE/MoS2/active layer interfaces. The long-term stability of the FOSCs is almost two times better than that of their counterparts with conventional organic HTL. By adding GQDs in the active layer, the PCE is further enhanced to 4.23% and is maintained at 89/82% of the original value after inner/outer repeated bending tests for 1000 cycles, respectively, indicating outstanding mechanical stability. These results suggest that GQDs are very useful for the active layer of FOSCs with better PCE and flexibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible organic solar cells (FOSCs) are highly attractive in view of low cost, light weight, and versatile usability [1-5]. Poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT:PSS) has been conventionally used as a hole transport layer (HTL) for organic solar cells (OSCs), but its acidity and hygroscopicity limit the long-term stability of the cells [6-8]. For improving the durability of FOSCs, it is important to find new materials for transport layers to replace PEDOT:PSS. Recently, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), well known as a two-dimensional semiconductor, have received strong attention as a promising candidate for an active- or a transport-layer in various optoelectronic device applications thanks to several advantages, one of which is adjustment of the energy band gap by the number of layers (Ln) [9-11]. Among TMDs, MoS2 is most intensively being used in optoelectronic devices such as light emitting diodes [12,13], photodetectors [14-16], and photovoltaic devices [17-19] because of its high mobility, relatively-large energy band gap, and chemical vapor deposition (CVD)-producibility [20-22]. Especially, high-quality, large-area CVD-grown MoS2 has been used as a transport layer in OSCs because MoS2 is chemically stable compared to traditional transport layers, thereby improving the durability of the cells [18].

Another critical issue in FOSCs is to enhance the flexibility of their transparent conductive electrodes (TCEs) for better durability of the device performance after repeated bending tests [23,24]. Therefore, it is necessary to find TCEs with low sheet resistance (Rs)/high transparency in the visible region as well as with high flexibility/elasticity. In particular, the selection of a TCE is crucial for solar cells because the photovoltaic performance is dominated by the amount of incident light. Among the TCE candidates, graphene (GR) TCE has been extensively employed for various kinds of flexible optoelectronic devices due to its excellent electrical/optical/stretchable properties [25,26], but high Rs of GR is the main obstacle to limit its practical applications. In a solar cell, the Rs of the TCE strongly affects the carrier collection, and so should be lowered as much as possible. To lower the Rs, a lot of efforts are being made by controlling the layer number of GR [27-29] and/or its doping concentration (nD) [28,30]. In our previous report, bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)-amide (TFSA) was successfully doped in GR for the p-type properties, thereby lowering the Rs with minimal reduction in the transmittance (T) and possible enhancement in the long-term stability [31,32].

Recently, there have been several reports on improving the PCE and stability by adding carbon nanodots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), or GR oxide to OSCs, but all of them were based on rigid substrates [33-35]. Unlike conventional metal nanomaterials, GQDs are known to be useful for eliminating the possibility of short circuit formation within the GQDs-bedded organic layer thanks to the sufficient band gap energy and peripheral hydrophilic groups. Until now, no studies were done on simultaneous use of GQDs and MoS2 as active layer and HTL, respectively in FOSCs. Here, we employ MoS2 HTL and TFSA-GR TCE for FOSCs and optimize their photovoltaic parameters with varying Ln and nD. GQDs are also added in the organic active layer to further enhance the performance of FOSCs with optimized Ln and nD. To demonstrate the flexible excellence of FOSCs, we perform inner/outer bending tests at a radius of curvature (d) with 4 mm.

Single-layer GR film was synthesized on 70-μm Cu foil (Wacopa, 99.8 purity) in a CVD (SNTEK model TCS5000). The Cu foil was heated up to 1000 °C under 325 sccm Ar/10 sccm H2 flow for 1 h, and this condition was maintained for 2 h. Subsequently, CH4 gas with a rate of flow of 10 sccm was sent into the chamber for 5 min. After the CH4 flow was stopped, the chamber was slowly cooled with a natural cooling rate of 5–20 °C/min. Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, Sigma-Aldrich)/chlorobenzene (0.46 g/10 mL, Sigma-Aldrich) was stirred at 60 °C for 10 h to prepare the PMMA solution. After dropping the PMMA solution onto the entire GR/Cu foil, it was spin-coated at 4500 rpm for 1 min, and then immediately heat-treated at 180 °C for 2 h. To separate the GR sheet from the substrate, the PMMA/GR/Cu stack was immersed in FeCl3 solution (Sigma-Aldrich) and the remaining PMMA/GR was then sufficiently washed in deionized (DI) water at 80 °C. After the PMMA/GR was transferred onto a polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate, it was dried on a hot plate at 100 °C for 30 min. The GR/PET stack was then obtained by removing the PMMA with acetone. To make TFSA-GR, TFSA solutions (10, 20, and 30 mM) were prepared by adjusting the amount of TFSA powder (Sigma-Aldrich) in nitromethane (Sigma-Aldrich). The TFSA solution was dropped on the entire GR surface, spin-coated at 2500 rpm for 1 min, and then heat-treated at 100 °C for 1 min.

MoS2 sheet was grown on 300 nm SiO2/Si substrate in a CVD furnace with a 2-inch-diameter horizontal-quartz-tube. A mixture powder of MoO3/NaCl (Sigma-Aldrich) and S powder were put in separate ceramic boats. The temperatures of the mixed powder/S powder were maintained at 750/150 °C, respectively under 5 sccm H2 and 50 sccm Ar flow, and the distance between the boats was fixed at 17 cm. The SiO2/Si substrate was placed on the top of the boat with the mixed powder. Multilayer MoS2 was prepared by increasing the amount of the mixed powder while the mixing ratio is fixed (MoO3:NaCl = 2:1). Details of the MoS2 growth was also described in our previous literature [36]. To obtain a MoS2 film, PMMA was coated on the MoS2 surface in the same manner as the GR transfer process. Subsequently, the PMMA/MoS2/SiO2/Si stack was immersed in a buffer oxide etching solution to separate the PMMA/MoS2 from the SiO2/Si substrate. The MoS2 sheet was then transferred onto the previously-prepared GR/PET substrate.

The blend solution was prepared by dissolving P3HT (25 mg) and PCBM (25 mg) powder in chlorobenzene (1 mL). For the active layer with GQDs, 0.02 wt% of GQDs was added to the blend solution. The GQDs of 5 nm average diameter used in this work were purchased from Dotz Nano Ltd. The synthesis method of the GQDs were detailed in previous publications [37,38]. Under a glove box filled with nitrogen atmosphere, the blend solution was dropped onto the MoS2 surface and spin coated at 700 rpm for 60 s. Subsequently, the device was treated by solvent annealing at 120 °C for 2 h and then followed by further annealing at 110 °C for 10 min. The counter electrode Al was deposited on the P3HT:PCBM surface using a thermal evaporator at 10−6 Torr. On the other hand, the FOSCs with GR TCE and PEDOT:PSS transport layer as a control sample were fabricated following similar processes.

The roughness and thickness of GR and MoS2 were analyzed by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Park systems model XE100). Raman spectra of GR and MoS2 sheets were measured in a spectroscopy system (NOST, HEDA) at room temperature using a 532 nm lase line. The T and absorbance of GR and MoS2 were measured from 300 to 900 nm for the samples transferred on quartz substrates by using ultraviolet–visible–near infrared spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary 5000). Rs and work function were averaged for the data measured at random positions by 4-probe van der Pau method (Dasol eng model FPP-HS8-40 K) and Kevin probe force microscopy (Park systems, model XE100), respectively. The measured work functions were corrected with reference to Au. The atomic bonding state of GR and MoS2 were checked by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra using Al kα line (1486.6 eV). The size and distribution of GQDs were analyzed through transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) with electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS). Electrical behaviors of the cells were evaluated by Keithley 2400 source meter. Photovoltaic parameters of the FOSCs were measured by a solar simulator (McScience model K201) under 1 Sun illumination (100 mWcm−2 AM 1.5G). The illumination area was fixed to 9 mm2. External quantum efficiency (EQE) was measured from 300 to 900 nm by a power source (450 W xenon lamp, Oriel L Apex illuminator, Newport) with a monochromator (Cornerstone 260, Newport), and then calibrated by a Si cell. Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS, Wonatech, model Zive Labs) of the FOSCs was measured in dark condition.

Fig. 1 a shows a schematic of a typical Al/P3HT:PCBM/MoS2/TFSA-GR solar cell. Root-mean-square roughness (Rq) of the GR was 0.33 and 0.41 nm for nD = 0 and 20 mM, respectively, as shown by AFM height profiles (Fig. S1a†), indicating negligible variation of the surface smoothness and uniformity even by the TFSA doping, unlike other kinds of dopants [32]. The surface of the GR was also shown to be almost uniform after doping by AFM images (Fig. S1a†). C1s and N 1 s XPS spectra were measured for pristine GR and TFSA-GR (20 mM). (Fig. S1b-c†), and then resolved into several molecular bonding components by a XPS fitting program. The C-N peak appeared at 285.5 and 399.4 eV in the C 1 s and N 1 s spectra, respectively for the TFSA-GR, but not in any spectra for the pristine GR. Another nitrogen-related –NH– peak was also identified at 400.9 eV in the N 1 s spectrum. These results suggest that the TFSA doping was successful [39]. The Raman G and 2D bands of the GR were blue-shifted by the TFSA doping (Fig. S1d†), due to the phonon softening by the charge transfer from the dopants to the GR, consistent with the previous results [31,32]. For effectively collecting charge carriers in the solar cell, it is very important to engineer the band alignment at the TFSA-GR TCE/MoS2 interface by controlling the work function of the TCE. As nD increased, the work function decreased monotonically from −4.54 to −4.90 eV, indicating p-type characteristics of the TFSA-GR TCE (Fig. S1e†). High T and low Rs are required for the TCEs of photovoltaic cells, thereby making light harvesting and carrier collection high. T (at λ = 550 nm) is 97.39, 97.18, 96.96, and 96.75% for nD = 0, 10, 20, and 30 mM, respectively, meaning only 1% reduction even at the highest nD (Fig. S1e†). The Rs showed a monotonic decrease: 560, 205, 175, and 167 Ω/sq for an increase of nD: 0, 10, 20, and 30 mM (Fig. S1f†). For evaluating the TCE performance, σDC (direct conductivity)/σop (optical conductivity) ratio was calculated using the formula: T = {1 + (Z0/2Rs)(σop/σDC)}, where Z0 is impedance of free space [40]. The σDC/σop of the TFSA-GR reached a maximum of 70 at nD = 20 mM (Fig. S1f†), indicating that the TCE is well suited for solar cell applications.

By AFM topographic images and height profiles, the CVD-grown MoS2 films were shown to be uniform and the Rq/thickness were estimated to be 0.84/1.72 nm and 2.48/7.34 nm for Ln = 2 and 8, respectively (Fig. S2a-b†). Mo 3d and S 2p peaks were clearly observed in XPS spectra of the MoS2 film at Ln = 2 (Fig. S2c†), also demonstrating their good quality. To closely check Ln of the MoS2 films, we analyzed thickness-dependent variations of the E1 2g (385 cm−1) and A1g (405 cm−1) Raman peaks (Fig. S2d†) [41]. The peak position differences between the E1 2g and A1g modes were estimated to be 19, 22, 25, and 27 cm−1 for the four samples, corresponding to Ln = 1, 2, 4, and 8, respectively [41]. The T of the MoS2 film was >75% in the visible light region for Ln ≤ 4, but it decreased sharply at Ln = 8 (Fig. S2e†). In contrast, Ln-dependent variation of the absorbance was opposite to that of T (Fig. S2e†), consistent with the previous literature [42]. The work function of the MoS2 sheet was measured to be −4.85, −4.96, −5.09, and −5.13 eV for Ln = 1, 2, 4, and 8, respectively (Fig. S2f†). The work functions of the TFSA-GR and MoS2 can be properly matched for the holes generated in the active layer (P3HT:PCBM) to efficiently move to the TFSA-GR TCE through the MoS2 transport layer. To systematically analyze the carrier transport, the band diagram of a typical FOSC was described based on the well-known band energies of the constituent materials, as shown in Fig. 1b. When light is irradiated on the cell, both electrons and holes generated in the active layer can move smoothly to both electrodes.

We fabricated and characterized the FOSCs for all combinations of nD = 0, 10, 20, and 30 mM and Ln = 1, 2, 4, and 8 (Fig. 1c and Fig. S3†). As an optimum case, Fig. 1c shows current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of the FOSCs for various Ln at nD = 20 mM. The extracted photovoltaic parameters: open circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF), and power conversion efficiency (PCE) are summarized in Table 1 . The PCE is the largest (3.56%) at Ln = 2. At Ln = 1, the PCE is reduced due to lower absorption (higher T, Fig. S2e†) in the visible region whilst at Ln > 2, the PCE is also lowered due to smaller T in the visible region, resulting in less amount of sunlight reaching the active layer. The PCE showed also a maximum (3.19 ± 0.37%) at Ln = 2 by the statistical deviation of the average PCEs for 10 samples at each Ln (Fig. S4†). To better analyze the Ln dependence, EQE spectra of the FOSCs were measured from 300 to 800 nm, as shown in Fig. 1d. All the cells exhibit broad EQE spectra in the wavelength ranging from 300 to 800 nm, with the greatest magnitude being at Ln = 2, resulting from the Ln-dependent trade-off correlation between T and work function of MoS2. The integrated EQE is known to have close relationship with Jsc [43], as also proved in Fig. 1d.

The photovoltaic parameters for other combinations of nD = 0, 10, and 30 mM/Ln = 1, 2, 4, and 8 were also extracted (Table S1†). As a typical case, the nD dependences of J-V curves and EQE spectra/correlation with Jsc were checked by varying nD at a fixed Ln of 2 (Fig S5†). All the properties were best at nD = 20 mM. For comparison, the FOSC with conventional transport layer PEDOT:PSS was fabricated as a control sample (Fig. S6†). The PEDOT:PSS cell showed a maximum PCE of 3.12%, smaller than that of the MoS2 cell. To further compare the long-term stability, the PCE for the two kinds of the FOSCs were monitored under N2 atmosphere for 500 h (Fig. S7†). The MoS2-/PEDOT:PSS-cells maintained about 68 (absolutely from 3.56 to 2.39%)/30% (absolutely from 3.12 to 0.94%) of each original PCE after 500 h. To clarify why the cell was degraded, the AFM images of the active layer were obtained on both HTLs at 0 and 500 h. (Fig. S7d†). Small pinholes were observed on the P3HT:PCBM/PEDOT:PSS, but almost not on the P3HT:PCBM/MoS2. These results indicate that the degradation of the PEDOT:PSS cell is much faster, resulting from the damage of the active layer by the acid and hydrophobic PEDOT:PSS [8,44,45]. The MoS2 layer is therefore recognized as a better transport layer for enhancing the efficiency as well as the long-term stability.

To clarify the mechanism for the best performance of the cells at Ln = 2 and nD = 20 mM, we analyzed dark current-voltage (I-V) behaviors at nD = 20 mM, as shown in Fig. 2 a, to extract shunt resistance (Rsh), ideality factor (n), and series resistance (Rse) as functions of Ln, based on well-known equations [46]. At low voltage (region I, V < 0.1 V), intermediate voltage (region II, V = 0.1–0.5 V), and high voltage (region III, V < 0.8 V), the I-V curve is usually characterized by Rsh, n, and Rse, respectively. Fig. 2b shows Ln-dependent behaviors of Rsh, n, and Rse. The Rsh is the largest (~925 Ohm-cm2) at Ln = 2–4, indicating smallest recombination. The n is the smallest (2.15) at Ln = 2, meaning best diode quality. Rse is minimized to 2.15 Ohm-cm2 at Ln = 2, resulting from the lowest resistance at the TCE/MoS2/ active layer interfaces, thereby making the carrier transport most smooth. Larger Rsh and smaller Rse usually induce higher FF. These analyses explain why the photovoltaic performance is best at Ln = 2 and nD = 20 mM.

As a next step, GQDs were added to the active layer (P3HT:PCBM) to further enhance the performance of the cells. The average diameter of the GQDs was estimated to be approximately 5 nm by analyzing the HRTEM image (Fig. S8a†). The existence of the GQDs was also confirmed by the EELS spectrum peaked at ~285 and ~291 eV, suggesting the π* and σ* states, respectively, derived from the GQDs (Fig. S8b†). Absorbance spectrum of the GQDs aqueous solution was peaked at 356 nm, consistent with the reported literature (Fig. S8c†) [47]. AFM was employed to see the difference on the surface of P3HT:PCBM layer with/without GQDs (Fig. S9†). The Rq of the active layers (P3HT:PCBM) with/without GQDs are 1.26 and 2.58 nm, respectively, indicating a big reduction of the Rq by including GQDs. Therefore, it is believed that the presence of GQDs will help to make the active layer smooth and uniform, thereby improving the device performance. Fig. 3 a shows J-V curves of the cells with/without GQDs. By adding the GQDs in the active layer, the PCE, Jsc, and FF were enhanced by about 20, 9, and 8%, respectively whilst the Voc was varied almost negligibly, as shown in Table 2 . Fig. 3b shows the EQE of the cells with/without GQDs. EQE was enhanced in the entire wavelength range by adding the GQDs due to their strong absorption effect [47]. The electrical parameters extracted from the dark I-V curves: Rsh, Rse, and n were all improved to 1227 Ohm-cm2, 2.53 Ohm-cm2, and 1.92, respectively, as shown in Table 2. To further understand why the PCE is improved by the inclusion of GQDs, we performed impedance spectroscopy measurements. Fig. 3d shows Nyquist plots of the devices with/without GQDs. Clearly, the charge transfer resistance (RCT) is smaller for the device with GQDs, resulting in the enhancement of the PCE. These results suggest that GQDs are very useful for improving the active layer of the OSCs.

Finally, we checked the possibility of the FOSCs with/without GQDs as a flexible power source. The mechanical stability of the FOSCs was evaluated by inner and outer bending tests, as shown in Fig. 4 a-b. Here, the d is 4 mm and the bending frequency is 0.5 Hz. A compressive or tensile stress is mostly applied to the TFSA-GR TCE/MoS2 transport layer in the FOSCs during inner and outer bending tests. Fig. 4c-d shows normalized PCE of the FOSCs during 1000 repeated inner/outer bending tests at d = 4 mm, resulting in only 11/18% loss of the original PCE, respectively. This bending performance is better than that of the FOSCs without GQDs, exhibiting loss of 14/22% of the initial PCE during inner/outer bending tests, respectively (Fig. S10†). To know why the FOSC was degraded by the repeated bending tests, the Rs of the TFSA-GR TCE/PET was measured throughout 1000 inner/outer bending cycles at d = 4 mm (Fig. S11†). After 1000 cycles under inner/outer bending tests, the relative Rs (ΔRs/Rs) of GR increased by 12/21%, respectively. To analyze the cause of the Rs increase, plan-view SEM images were obtained on the GR sheet before/after outer bending tests. The cracks were shown on the surface of GR after the bending tests, thereby increasing the Rs, resulting in the reduction of the efficiency, consistent with the previous literature [24,48].

FOSCs were successfully fabricated by adjusting Ln and nD. The FOSCs with a structure of TFSA-GR/MoS2/P3HT:PCBM/Al exhibited maximum PCE of 3.56% at Ln = 2/nD = 20 mM and maintained 68% of the original PCE value after they were kept in air for 500 h. In comparison, the FOSCs employing traditionally-well-known PEDOT:PSS HTL showed maximum PCE of 3.12% and 70% reduction in the original PCE after 500 h. These results suggest that the PCE and long-term stability are remarkably enhanced by the use of MoS2 as a HTL due to the lowest resistance at the TCE/MoS2/active layer interfaces. Adding GQDs to the active layer further enhanced the PCE by about 20% (absolutely from 3.56 to 4.23%) and improved the mechanical stability (more than 80% of the original PCE remained after the bending tests).

D.H. Shin: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Resources. C.W. Jang: Methodology, Software, Visualization. J.S. Ko: Data curation, Methodology. S.H. Choi: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (No. 2020R1A2C2004376).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148155.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrathin and lightweight organic solar cells with high flexibility

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Kaltenbrunner, M.S. White, E.D. Głowacki, T. Sekitani, T. Someya, N.S. Sariciftci, S. Bauer, Ultrathin and lightweight organic solar cells with high flexibility, Nat. Commun. 3 (2012) 770.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Large scale deployment of polymer solar cells on land, on sea and in the air

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Espinosa, M. Hösel, M. Jørgensen, F.C. Krebs, Large scale deployment of polymer solar cells on land, on sea and in the air, Energy Environ. Sci. 7 (2014) 855–866.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inverted indium-tin-oxide-free cone-shaped polymer solar cells for light trapping

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhen, K. Li, Z. Huang, Z. Tang, R. Wu, G. Li, X. Liu, F. Zhang, Inverted indium-tin-oxide-free cone-shaped polymer solar cells for light trapping, Appl. Phys. Lett. 100 (2012) 213901.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stretchable, elastic materials and devices for solar energy conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.J. Lipomi, Z. Bao, Stretchable, elastic materials and devices for solar energy conversion, Energy Environ. Sci. 4 (2011) 3314–3328.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface-engineered graphene quantum dots incorporated into polymer layers for high performance organic photovoltaics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.K. Kim, S.J. Kim, M.J. Park, S. Bae, S.-P. Cho, Q.G. Du, D.H. Wang, J.H. Park, B.H. Hong, Surface-engineered graphene quantum dots incorporated into polymer layers for high performance organic photovoltaics, Sci. Rep. 5 (2015) 14276.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Atomically thin-layered molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) for bulk-heterojunction solar cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface engineering of ITO electrode with a functional polymer for PEDOT:PSS-free organic solar cells
